title: IFU Workflow
text:
library(FITSio)
library(SpectralUnmix)

X <- readFITS("cube.fits")
Mat <- cube_to_matrix(X)
Mat[!is.finite(Mat)] <- 0
Mat <- pmax(Mat, 0)

title: IFU Workflow
text:
library(FITSio)
library(SpectralUnmix)

X <- readFITS("cube.fits")
Mat <- cube_to_matrix(X)
Mat[!is.finite(Mat)] <- 0
Mat <- pmax(Mat, 0)

title: IFU Workflow
text:
Fit and inspect

fit <- spectral_unmix(Mat, k = 5, lr = 0.01, niter = 5000)

plot(fit, type = "spectra")
plot(fit, type = "maps", nx = dim(X$imDat)[1], ny = dim(X$imDat)[2])
plot_reconstruction(fit, Mat, n = 6)

Example spatial maps from the bundled synthetic cube:

title: IFU Workflow
text:
Reconstruction outputs

maps <- predict(fit, type = "spatial")
cube_hat <- predict(
  fit,
  type = "cube",
  nx = dim(X$imDat)[1],
  ny = dim(X$imDat)[2]
)
resid <- residuals(
  fit,
  x = Mat,
  nx = dim(X$imDat)[1],
  ny = dim(X$imDat)[2]
)

title: IFU Workflow
text:
Metadata and redshift
When the input to cube_to_matrix() is a FITS-like list object, non-image entries are carried forward as metadata. That metadata can be recovered from the fitted object or reconstructed cube with cube_metadata().

fit <- spectral_unmix(Mat, k = 5, lr = 0.01, niter = 5000)
cube_metadata(fit)
cube_metadata(cube_hat)

If redshift information is stored in the FITS-side metadata, it will now be preserved as metadata and can be carried into fitted and reconstructed outputs.

title: Getting Started
text:
library(SpectralUnmix)
demo <- coelho_demo_spectra()

title: Getting Started
text:
Load the bundled spectra
The bundled Coelho demo provides four normalized stellar spectra on a common wavelength grid. It is the simplest fully reproducible entry point for the package.

dim(demo$matrix)

[1]    4 2250

demo$metadata

           id                         file  teff logg feh
1  cool_giant t03000_g-0.5_p00p00_sed.fits  3000 -0.5 0.0
2 solar_dwarf t05750_g+4.5_p00p04_sed.fits  5750  4.5 0.0
3      a_star t09000_g+4.5_p00p00_sed.fits  9000  4.5 0.0
4    hot_star t18000_g+4.5_p02p00_sed.fits 18000  4.5 0.2

title: Getting Started
text:
Fit the model

fit <- spectral_unmix(
  demo$matrix,
  k = 4,
  lambda_smooth = 0,
  niter = 400,
  lr = 0.03
)

title: Getting Started
text:
Inspect the result

print(fit)
summary(fit)
basis(fit)
coef(fit)
fit$niter_run
fit$converged

title: Getting Started
text:
Plot the spectra

plot(fit, type = "spectra", wavelength = demo$wavelength)
plot_reconstruction(
  fit,
  demo$matrix,
  wavelength = demo$wavelength
)

Recovered-versus-observed view for the bundled Coelho sample:

title: Getting Started
text:
What the outputs mean

fit$spectra contains the recovered component spectra.
basis(fit) returns those components as wavelength-by-component columns.
fit$reconstruction is the model approximation A %*% S.
fit$loss stores the optimization history.
fit$niter_run reports how many optimization steps were actually used.
fit$converged reports whether early stopping triggered before niter.

title: Getting Started
text:
Larger library subset
For a denser stellar-library example, use the curated Coelho subset bundled with the package.

stellar_lib <- coelho_stellar_subset()
dim(stellar_lib$matrix)

[1]  100 2250

table(stellar_lib$metadata$type)


  a_f_star cool_dwarf cool_giant   hot_star solar_like 
        20         20         20         20         20 



fit_lib <- spectral_unmix(
  stellar_lib$matrix,
  k = 5,
  lambda_smooth = 0.001,
  niter = 500,
  lr = 0.03
)

plot(fit_lib, type = "spectra", wavelength = stellar_lib$wavelength)

title: Getting Started
text:
IFU note
The package still supports cube workflows through cube_to_matrix(), matrix_to_cube(), predict(type = "cube"), and component_map(). Those helpers are kept in the API, but the website examples stay focused on the bundled Coelho data so the documentation remains short and reproducible.

title: Reference
text:
spectral_unmix()
summary()
print()

The fitted object also exposes optimization diagnostics through loss, niter_run, and converged.

title: SpectralUnmix
text: SpectralUnmix is an R package for smooth non-negative spectral unmixing of hyperspectral datasets and astronomical IFU cubes.

title: SpectralUnmix
text:
Model
The package solves
\[
X \approx A S
\]
where X is a spaxel-by-wavelength matrix, A contains spatial abundances, and S contains component spectra.

title: SpectralUnmix
text:
Minimal workflow

flowchart LR
  A["Coelho demo spectra"] --> B["coelho_demo_spectra()"]
  B --> C["spectral matrix X"]
  C --> D["spectral_unmix()"]
  D --> E["component spectra"]
  D --> F["reconstruction"]
  E --> G["basis() / plot(type = 'spectra')"]
  F --> H["plot_reconstruction()"]




flowchart LR
  A["Coelho demo spectra"] --> B["coelho_demo_spectra()"]
  B --> C["spectral matrix X"]
  C --> D["spectral_unmix()"]
  D --> E["component spectra"]
  D --> F["reconstruction"]
  E --> G["basis() / plot(type = 'spectra')"]
  F --> H["plot_reconstruction()"]

title: SpectralUnmix
text:
Interpretation
Each input spectrum is modeled as a non-negative combination of a small number of latent spectral components. In the Coelho demo, the goal is not spatial mapping but clean recovery of shared spectral structure from a compact library.

title: SpectralUnmix
text:
Example output
The figure below shows the Coelho-based benchmark used in the validation page. The comparison is made on a controlled mixture benchmark built from the bundled stellar spectra.

title: SpectralUnmix
text:
Reproducible example
library(SpectralUnmix)

demo <- coelho_demo_spectra()

fit <- spectral_unmix(
  demo$matrix,
  k = 4,
  lambda_smooth = 0,
  niter = 400,
  lr = 0.03
)

plot(fit, type = "spectra", wavelength = demo$wavelength)
plot_reconstruction(fit, demo$matrix, wavelength = demo$wavelength)

title: SpectralUnmix
text:
Main functions

spectral_unmix() fits the model.
basis() and coef() provide NMF-style accessors.
fitted(), predict(), and residuals() provide standard model methods.
plot() and plot_reconstruction() provide quick diagnostics.
cube_to_matrix() and matrix_to_cube() remain available for IFU workflows.

title: SpectralUnmix
text:
Validation
The validation page documents two complementary checks:

benchmark tests against NMF::nmf() for the baseline factorization behavior
a Coelho-based basis benchmark that compares normalized recovered components against known reference spectra

title: SpectralUnmix
text:
Installation
# install.packages("remotes")
remotes::install_github("RafaelSdeSouza/SpectralUnmix")
torch must be available in the local R installation.

title: Validation
Validation
text:
Coelho-first validation
The validation story on the website is centered on the bundled Coelho spectra. That keeps the example aligned with the research note while still checking the core matrix factorization behavior of spectral_unmix().

title: Validation
text:
Coelho basis benchmark
The figure below uses the bundled Coelho demo spectra as the true basis, then generates a synthetic mixture matrix from them. That gives us a controlled comparison in which the target basis is known and both SpectralUnmix and NMF::nmf() can be compared on the same problem.

if (file.exists("images/coelho-basis-benchmark.png")) {
  knitr::include_graphics("images/coelho-basis-benchmark.png")
} else {
  cat("Coelho benchmark figure not found in `site/images/coelho-basis-benchmark.png`.\n")
}









if (!is.null(coelho_bench)) {
  coelho_bench$reconstruction_mse <- signif(coelho_bench$reconstruction_mse, 4)
  coelho_bench$basis_mse <- signif(coelho_bench$basis_mse, 4)
  knitr::kable(coelho_bench, caption = "Coelho basis benchmark summary.")
} else {
  cat("Coelho benchmark summary CSV not found.\n")
}


Coelho basis benchmark summary.


method
reconstruction_mse
basis_mse
iterations
converged




SpectralUnmix
1e-07
0.001256
2000
FALSE


NMF
1e-07
0.001300
NA
NA





For this benchmark, the meaningful comparison is after component matching and normalization. The curves should lie close to one another, but exact equality is not expected because:

NMF solutions are only identifiable up to permutation and scaling.
SpectralUnmix and NMF::nmf() use different optimizers.
once smoothness regularization is turned on, the two objectives are no longer the same.

title: Validation
text:
What is checked
The package test suite currently verifies three things:

spectral_unmix() recovers a noise-free low-rank factorization with small reconstruction and component-spectrum error.
spectral_unmix() is competitive with NMF::nmf(method = "lee") on the same toy problem after matching fitted components to the reference ordering.
early stopping and convergence bookkeeping are exposed through loss, niter_run, and converged.

These checks live in:

tests/testthat/test-vanilla-nmf-reference.R

title: Validation
text:
Why compare to NMF
The NMF package is a natural baseline for the unregularized matrix factorization problem. Matching that reference behavior on a simple benchmark gives us confidence that the torch-based optimizer in SpectralUnmix is recovering the expected NMF structure before smoothness regularization or IFU data handling are layered on top.

title: Validation
text:
Running the validation locally
devtools::test(filter = "vanilla-nmf")
The benchmark tests require both torch and NMF. If either package is not available in the local R environment, the corresponding tests are skipped.

title: Validation
text:
Interpreting the diagnostics

fit$loss is the optimization history.
fit$niter_run is the number of iterations actually executed.
fit$converged indicates whether early stopping triggered before the maximum iteration budget.

For practical work, the benchmark is intended as a sanity check. Good behavior here means the core factorization engine is behaving as expected; difficult real-data fits can then be investigated as modeling or preprocessing issues rather than as a basic failure of the package optimizer.
